Patients typically\npresent at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients\nare directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform\nclinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway.\nModule 1 Recommended Practices \n1.1 Initial evaluation\n1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway,\nbreathing, and circulation\n1.2 Initial examinations\n1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to\ndetermine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a\nstandardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke)\n1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological\nsymptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom\nonset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be\ninitiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who\nmeet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular\ntherapy\n1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute\nstroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging\n(non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less\nthan twenty five minutes)\n1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible\nfor acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and\nmultiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered.\n1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care\nprovider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of\nthe brain\n1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and\nother acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment\n1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis \n1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work:\n• CBC\n• electrolytes\n• creatinine\n• glucose\n• INR\n• partial thromboplastin time\n• troponin test (if clinically indicated)\n1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia\n1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have\ntheir swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside\ntesting protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating\noral medication, fluid, or food\n1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours\nshould be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be\nscreened when clinically appropriate\n1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia\nor pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of\ntheir swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or\nappropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability\nand the required consistency of diet and fluids\n1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies\n1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the\nED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary\nprevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)\n1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients\n1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke\n1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients\n1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or\nischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and\nneck arteries as soon as possible\n1.7 Initial examinations for ICH\n1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH\nshould be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated\nimmediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management\n1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions\nabout anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and\nINR\n1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as\npossible\nAbbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke\nManagement; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale;\nCT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT,\nEndovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI,\nmagnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke\nScale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient\nischemic attack.\n\nThe following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.\n\nModule 1 Implementation Considerations\nGeneral considerations\n• Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as\npossible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute\nneurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a\npotential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy (EVT)\n• Regional planning for access to acute stroke EVT should consider the following:\no The importance of rapid triage, assessment and brain imaging (including parallel vascular imaging) of acute\nstroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT.\no Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements\no Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall\nOntario.\no Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process.\n• Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a\ngreater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre\n• Where feasible, development of processes and protocols to facilitate EMS to divert patients to regional or district\nstroke and /Endovascular Treatment centres if there is suspicion of stroke\n• The process for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better\nestablished to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information\n• Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks\nacross the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care\n• Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical\nredirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and\nmanagement.\n• Upon receiving EMS prenotification, the receiving hospital’s acute stroke team should be contacted and called to the\nED (for appropriate patients)\n• Standardized stroke assessment and treatment protocols/tools should be developed and used in all Ontario hospital\nEDs\n• Access should be increased in all hospitals to either stroke physician experts or radiologists who can interpret brain\nimaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time\nof less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to needle time of 30 mins. of patient arrival for\nappropriate patients\n• Sufficient human resources capacity should be ensured so that patients can be diagnosed and treated in a timely\nmanner\n• To facilitate early assessment, hospital-level CTAS I or CTAS II access to diagnostic imaging should be established\nfor suspected stroke patients\n• Rapid turnaround times for lab work should be negotiated in hospitals that provide tPA thrombolysis for ischemic\nstroke patients to facilitate rapid assessment and treatment\n• Efforts to raise public awareness about the symptoms of stroke and when to contact 911 should continue to be\nenhanced and funded\n• Capacity planning to ensure access to CT/CTA should include:\no Training for CT technologists\no Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol\no Human resource availability\no Communication processes with EVT Centre experts\nAbbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue\nplasminogen activator.', 'suggestions': "Problem: The parallelGateway with id='parallelGateway1' is logically unreasonable because the downstream exclusiveGateway with id='exclusiveGateway5' can only be reached after both parallel branches are completed. This means the diagnosis-specific pathway based on brain imaging, especially the ICH emergency pathway, may be delayed by ECG, blood work, or chest x-ray activities in branch id='branch9'.\n\nSuggested modification: Move the exclusiveGateway with id='exclusiveGateway5' into the imaging branch id='branch1', placing it immediately after imaging interpretation activities id='activity6_1' and id='activity6_2'. Keep ECG, lab work, troponin decision, and chest x-ray in parallel as supportive tasks, but do not make completion of those tasks a prerequisite for entering the ICH or ischemic/TIA pathway.\n\nReason: According to the recommendations, brain imaging must be interpreted immediately, ICH must be treated as a medical emergency, and ECG/chest x-ray should not delay acute treatment. In the current design, the synchronization effect of parallelGateway1 incorrectly forces the process to wait for non-decisive parallel tasks before proceeding to urgent post-imaging clinical decisions.", 'format': "\nWhen the process starts, please output ''\nAn activity is represented as follows:\n\nThe exclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe parallel gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n", 'examples': ' '} |

| **assistant_role_prompt** | You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer. You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader. You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway. Here is a new customer's task: {task}. To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs. |
| **user_role_prompt** | You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer. You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process. Here is a new customer's task: {task}. To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs. |
| **task_prompt** | Module 1: Early Assessment This module identifies best practices for the early assessment of suspected stroke. Patients typically present at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients are directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform clinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway. Module 1 Recommended Practices  1.1 Initial evaluation 1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway, breathing, and circulation 1.2 Initial examinations 1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to determine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a standardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke) 1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be initiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who meet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular therapy 1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute stroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging (non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less than twenty five minutes) 1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible for acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered. 1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care provider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of the brain 1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and other acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment 1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis  1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work: • CBC • electrolytes • creatinine • glucose • INR • partial thromboplastin time • troponin test (if clinically indicated) 1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia 1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have their swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside testing protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating oral medication, fluid, or food 1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours should be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be screened when clinically appropriate 1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia or pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of their swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or appropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability and the required consistency of diet and fluids 1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the ED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients 1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke 1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients 1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or ischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and neck arteries as soon as possible 1.7 Initial examinations for ICH 1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH should be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated immediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management 1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions about anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and INR 1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as possible Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale; CT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT, Endovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI, magnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke Scale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 1 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as possible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a potential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy (EVT) • Regional planning for access to acute stroke EVT should consider the following: o The importance of rapid triage, assessment and brain imaging (including parallel vascular imaging) of acute stroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT. o Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements o Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall Ontario. o Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process. • Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a greater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre • Where feasible, development of processes and protocols to facilitate EMS to divert patients to regional or district stroke and /Endovascular Treatment centres if there is suspicion of stroke • The process for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better established to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information • Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks across the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care • Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical redirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and management. • Upon receiving EMS prenotification, the receiving hospital’s acute stroke team should be contacted and called to the ED (for appropriate patients) • Standardized stroke assessment and treatment protocols/tools should be developed and used in all Ontario hospital EDs • Access should be increased in all hospitals to either stroke physician experts or radiologists who can interpret brain imaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time of less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to needle time of 30 mins. of patient arrival for appropriate patients • Sufficient human resources capacity should be ensured so that patients can be diagnosed and treated in a timely manner • To facilitate early assessment, hospital-level CTAS I or CTAS II access to diagnostic imaging should be established for suspected stroke patients • Rapid turnaround times for lab work should be negotiated in hospitals that provide tPA thrombolysis for ischemic stroke patients to facilitate rapid assessment and treatment • Efforts to raise public awareness about the symptoms of stroke and when to contact 911 should continue to be enhanced and funded • Capacity planning to ensure access to CT/CTA should include: o Training for CT technologists o Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol o Human resource availability o Communication processes with EVT Centre experts Abbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue plasminogen activator. |

[2026-07-04 23:13:56 INFO] Process design reviewer: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 1: Early Assessment
This module identifies best practices for the early assessment of suspected stroke. Patients typically
present at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients
are directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform
clinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway.
Module 1 Recommended Practices 
1.1 Initial evaluation
1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway,
breathing, and circulation
1.2 Initial examinations
1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to
determine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a
standardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke)
1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological
symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom
onset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be
initiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who
meet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular
therapy
1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute
stroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging
(non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less
than twenty five minutes)
1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible
for acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and
multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered.
1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care
provider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of
the brain
1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and
other acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment
1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis 
1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work:
• CBC
• electrolytes
• creatinine
• glucose
• INR
• partial thromboplastin time
• troponin test (if clinically indicated)
1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia
1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have
their swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside
testing protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating
oral medication, fluid, or food
1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours
should be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be
screened when clinically appropriate
1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia
or pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of
their swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or
appropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability
and the required consistency of diet and fluids
1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the
ED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients
1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke
1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients
1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or
ischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and
neck arteries as soon as possible
1.7 Initial examinations for ICH
1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH
should be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated
immediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management
1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions
about anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and
INR
1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as
possible
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale;
CT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT,
Endovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI,
magnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke
Scale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient
ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as
possible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute
neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a
potential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy (EVT)
• Regional planning for access to acute stroke EVT should consider the following:
o The importance of rapid triage, assessment and brain imaging (including parallel vascular imaging) of acute
stroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT.
o Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements
o Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall
Ontario.
o Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process.
• Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a
greater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre
• Where feasible, development of processes and protocols to facilitate EMS to divert patients to regional or district
stroke and /Endovascular Treatment centres if there is suspicion of stroke
• The process for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better
established to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information
• Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks
across the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care
• Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical
redirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and
management.
• Upon receiving EMS prenotification, the receiving hospital’s acute stroke team should be contacted and called to the
ED (for appropriate patients)
• Standardized stroke assessment and treatment protocols/tools should be developed and used in all Ontario hospital
EDs
• Access should be increased in all hospitals to either stroke physician experts or radiologists who can interpret brain
imaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time
of less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to needle time of 30 mins. of patient arrival for
appropriate patients
• Sufficient human resources capacity should be ensured so that patients can be diagnosed and treated in a timely
manner
• To facilitate early assessment, hospital-level CTAS I or CTAS II access to diagnostic imaging should be established
for suspected stroke patients
• Rapid turnaround times for lab work should be negotiated in hospitals that provide tPA thrombolysis for ischemic
stroke patients to facilitate rapid assessment and treatment
• Efforts to raise public awareness about the symptoms of stroke and when to contact 911 should continue to be
enhanced and funded
• Capacity planning to ensure access to CT/CTA should include:
o Training for CT technologists
o Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol
o Human resource availability
o Communication processes with EVT Centre experts
Abbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue
plasminogen activator..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

process text: 
<process>
  <activity role='ED or acute care team' action='identify' objects='suspected stroke presentation and urgent stroke triage priority' id='activity1_1'/>
  <activity role='ED or acute care team' action='activate' objects='acute stroke protocol and notify the stroke team' id='activity1_2'/>
  <activity role='clinical team' action='conduct rapid initial evaluation of' objects='airway, breathing, and circulation' id='activity2'/>
  <activity role='clinical team' action='assess' objects='focal neurological deficits' id='activity3_1'/>
  <activity role='clinical team' action='score' objects='stroke severity using a standardized stroke scale such as NIHSS or CNS' id='activity3_2'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelGateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='stroke team' action='determine' objects='symptom onset time and acute stroke treatment time window' id='activity4'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway1'>
        <branch condition='presenting within the acute stroke treatment time window' id='branch2'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway7'>
            <branch condition='non-contrast CT is selected as the initial brain imaging modality' id='branch20'>
              <activity role='imaging team' action='perform rapid' objects='non-contrast CT of the brain' id='activity5_1'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='MRI is selected as the initial brain imaging modality' id='branch21'>
              <activity role='imaging team' action='perform rapid' objects='MRI of the brain' id='activity5_2'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='imaging team' action='perform rapid' objects='CTA with door-to-CT or CTA less than 25 minutes' id='activity5_3'/>
          <activity role='qualified imaging expert' action='interpret immediately' objects='CT or MRI of the brain' id='activity6_1'/>
          <activity role='qualified imaging expert' action='interpret immediately' objects='CTA for vascular findings' id='activity6_2'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway2'>
            <branch condition='acute ischemic stroke is clinically eligible for acute stroke treatments' id='branch3'>
              <activity role='stroke physician or imaging team' action='consider' objects='advanced CT imaging including CT and multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT' id='activity7'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='acute ischemic stroke is not clinically eligible for acute stroke treatments' id='branch4'>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='continue assessment without' objects='advanced acute-treatment CT imaging' id='activity8'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway3'>
            <branch condition='potentially disabling acute neurological symptoms are within 6.0 hours of onset or treatment can be initiated within a 12-hour window from onset and clinical and imaging criteria are met' id='branch5'>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='contact' objects='stroke physician and EVT centre through CritiCall Ontario for potential endovascular therapy' id='activity9_1'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway8'>
                <branch condition='EVT can be provided at the current site' id='branch22'>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='proceed with' objects='on-site endovascular therapy pathway' id='activity9_2'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='EVT requires transfer to another EVT centre' id='branch23'>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='initiate' objects='transfer or drip-and-ship pathway to an EVT centre' id='activity9_3'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='EVT timing, clinical criteria, or imaging criteria are not met' id='branch6'>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='continue without' objects='endovascular therapy consideration' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='presenting beyond 48 hours with a TIA or ischemic stroke' id='branch7'>
          <activity role='stroke team or imaging team' action='perform as soon as possible' objects='vascular imaging of the brain and neck arteries' id='activity11'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='presentation does not meet the acute treatment window branch or the beyond-48-hours TIA-or-ischemic-stroke branch' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='continue clinician-directed assessment of' objects='stroke presentation and imaging needs' id='activity12'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch9'>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='perform' objects='ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and other acute arrhythmias without delaying acute treatment' id='activity13'/>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='obtain' objects='CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, glucose, INR, and partial thromboplastin time' id='activity14_1'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway9'>
        <branch condition='troponin test is clinically indicated' id='branch24'>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='obtain' objects='troponin test' id='activity14_2'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='troponin test is not clinically indicated' id='branch25'>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='continue without obtaining' objects='troponin test' id='activity14_3'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway4'>
        <branch condition='chest x-ray is clinically required' id='branch10'>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='obtain' objects='chest x-ray without delaying assessment for thrombolysis' id='activity15'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='chest x-ray is not clinically required' id='branch11'>
          <activity role='clinical team' action='continue' objects='thrombolysis assessment without chest x-ray' id='activity16'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway5'>
    <branch condition='brain imaging confirms acute intracerebral hemorrhage' id='branch12'>
      <activity role='stroke team' action='recognize' objects='acute intracerebral hemorrhage as a medical emergency' id='activity17_1'/>
      <activity role='stroke team' action='arrange' objects='immediate evaluation by a physician with expertise in stroke management' id='activity17_2'/>
      <activity role='stroke physician' action='ask about and review' objects='anticoagulant therapy, platelet count, PTT, and INR' id='activity18'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway6'>
        <branch condition='patient with ICH is selected for additional vascular imaging' id='branch13'>
          <activity role='imaging team' action='perform as soon as possible' objects='CTA or MRA' id='activity19'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient with ICH is not selected for additional vascular imaging' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='continue' objects='ICH pathway without CTA or MRA' id='activity20'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='brain imaging does not confirm acute intracerebral hemorrhage' id='branch15'>
      <activity role='stroke team' action='continue' objects='ischemic stroke or TIA pathway based on clinical findings and imaging' id='activity21'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <activity role='clinical team' action='make' objects='patient NPO initially' id='activity22'/>
  <inclusiveGateway id='inclusiveGateway1'>
    <branch condition='patient with stroke is alert and oral medication, fluid, or food is about to be initiated' id='branch17'>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='screen swallowing ability using' objects='a simple, valid, reliable bedside testing protocol before oral medication, fluid, or food' id='activity23'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient with stroke is not alert within the first 24 hours' id='branch18'>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='monitor closely and screen swallowing ability when' objects='clinically appropriate timing within the first 24 hours' id='activity24'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient with stroke has features indicating dysphagia or pulmonary aspiration' id='branch19'>
      <activity role='speech-language pathologist or appropriately trained specialist' action='perform full clinical assessment of' objects='swallowing ability' id='activity25'/>
      <activity role='speech-language pathologist or appropriately trained specialist' action='advise on' objects='swallowing ability and the required consistency of diet and fluids' id='activity26'/>
    </branch>
  </inclusiveGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway10'>
    <branch condition='patient is admitted to hospital' id='branch26'>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='provide' objects='appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies for admitted patients' id='activity27_1'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient is discharged from the ED' id='branch27'>
      <activity role='clinical team' action='provide' objects='appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies for patients discharged from the ED' id='activity27_2'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
suggestion:
Problem: The `parallelGateway` with id='parallelGateway1' is logically unreasonable because the downstream `exclusiveGateway` with id='exclusiveGateway5' can only be reached after **both** parallel branches are completed. This means the diagnosis-specific pathway based on brain imaging, especially the ICH emergency pathway, may be delayed by ECG, blood work, or chest x-ray activities in branch id='branch9'.

Suggested modification: Move the `exclusiveGateway` with id='exclusiveGateway5' into the imaging branch id='branch1', placing it immediately after imaging interpretation activities id='activity6_1' and id='activity6_2'. Keep ECG, lab work, troponin decision, and chest x-ray in parallel as supportive tasks, but do not make completion of those tasks a prerequisite for entering the ICH or ischemic/TIA pathway.

Reason: According to the recommendations, brain imaging must be interpreted immediately, ICH must be treated as a medical emergency, and ECG/chest x-ray should not delay acute treatment. In the current design, the synchronization effect of `parallelGateway1` incorrectly forces the process to wait for non-decisive parallel tasks before proceeding to urgent post-imaging clinical decisions.
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

[2026-07-04 23:14:36 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Process design reviewer on : ProcessReview, turn 0**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 1: Early Assessment
This module identifies best practices for the early assessment of suspected stroke. Patients typically
present at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients
are directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform
clinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway.
Module 1 Recommended Practices 
1.1 Initial evaluation
1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway,
breathing, and circulation
1.2 Initial examinations
1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to
determine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a
standardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke)
1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological
symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom
onset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be
initiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who
meet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular
therapy
1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute
stroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging
(non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less
than twenty five minutes)
1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible
for acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and
multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered.
1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care
provider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of
the brain
1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and
other acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment
1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis 
1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work:
• CBC
• electrolytes
• creatinine
• glucose
• INR
• partial thromboplastin time
• troponin test (if clinically indicated)
1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia
1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have
their swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside
testing protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating
oral medication, fluid, or food
1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours
should be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be
screened when clinically appropriate
1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia
or pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of
their swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or
appropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability
and the required consistency of diet and fluids
1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the
ED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients
1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke
1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients
1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or
ischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and
neck arteries as soon as possible
1.7 Initial examinations for ICH
1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH
should be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated
immediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management
1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions
about anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and
INR
1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as
possible
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale;
CT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT,
Endovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI,
magnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke
Scale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient
ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as
possible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute
neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a
potential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy (EVT)
• Regional planning for access to acute stroke EVT should consider the following:
o The importance of rapid triage, assessment and brain imaging (including parallel vascular imaging) of acute
stroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT.
o Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements
o Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall
Ontario.
o Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process.
• Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a
greater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre
• Where feasible, development of processes and protocols to facilitate EMS to divert patients to regional or district
stroke and /Endovascular Treatment centres if there is suspicion of stroke
• The process for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better
established to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information
• Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks
across the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care
• Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical
redirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and
management.
• Upon receiving EMS prenotification, the receiving hospital’s acute stroke team should be contacted and called to the
ED (for appropriate patients)
• Standardized stroke assessment and treatment protocols/tools should be developed and used in all Ontario hospital
EDs
• Access should be increased in all hospitals to either stroke physician experts or radiologists who can interpret brain
imaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time
of less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to needle time of 30 mins. of patient arrival for
appropriate patients
• Sufficient human resources capacity should be ensured so that patients can be diagnosed and treated in a timely
manner
• To facilitate early assessment, hospital-level CTAS I or CTAS II access to diagnostic imaging should be established
for suspected stroke patients
• Rapid turnaround times for lab work should be negotiated in hospitals that provide tPA thrombolysis for ischemic
stroke patients to facilitate rapid assessment and treatment
• Efforts to raise public awareness about the symptoms of stroke and when to contact 911 should continue to be
enhanced and funded
• Capacity planning to ensure access to CT/CTA should include:
o Training for CT technologists
o Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol
o Human resource availability
o Communication processes with EVT Centre experts
Abbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue
plasminogen activator..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

                  <activity role='stroke team' action='continue' objects='ICH pathway without CTA or MRA' id='activity20'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='brain imaging does not confirm acute intracerebral hemorrhage' id='branch15'>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='continue' objects='ischemic stroke or TIA pathway based on clinical findings and imaging' id='activity21'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusiveGateway2'>
                <branch condition='acute ischemic stroke is clinically eligible for acute stroke treatments' id='branch3'>

            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>

| **task_prompt** | Module 1: Early Assessment This module identifies best practices for the early assessment of suspected stroke. Patients typically present at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients are directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform clinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway. Module 1 Recommended Practices  1.1 Initial evaluation 1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway, breathing, and circulation 1.2 Initial examinations 1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to determine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a standardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke) 1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be initiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who meet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular therapy 1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute stroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging (non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less than twenty five minutes) 1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible for acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and multiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered. 1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care provider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of the brain 1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and other acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment 1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis  1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work: • CBC • electrolytes • creatinine • glucose • INR • partial thromboplastin time • troponin test (if clinically indicated) 1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia 1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have their swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside testing protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating oral medication, fluid, or food 1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours should be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be screened when clinically appropriate 1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia or pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of their swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or appropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability and the required consistency of diet and fluids 1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the ED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients 1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke 1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients 1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or ischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and neck arteries as soon as possible 1.7 Initial examinations for ICH 1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH should be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated immediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management 1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions about anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and INR 1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as possible Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale; CT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT, Endovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI, magnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke Scale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. Module 1 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as possible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a potential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy (EVT) • Regional planning for access to acute stroke EVT should consider the following: o The importance of rapid triage, assessment and brain imaging (including parallel vascular imaging) of acute stroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT. o Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements o Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall Ontario. o Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process. • Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a greater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre • Where for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better established to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information • Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks across the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care • Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical redirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and management. • Upon receiving EMS prenotification, the receiving hospital’s acute stroke team should be contacted and called to the ED (for appropriate patients) • Standardized stroke assessment and treatment protocols/tools should be developed and used in all Ontario hospital EDs • Access should be increased in all hospitals to either stroke physician experts or radiologists who can interpret brain imaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time of less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to needle time of 30 mins. of patient arrival for appropriate patients • Sufficient human resources capacity should be ensured so that patients can turnaround times for lab work should be negotiated in hospitals that provide tPA thrombolysis for ischemic stroke patients to facilitate rapid assessment and treatment • Efforts to raise public o Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol o Human resource availability o Communication processes with EVT Centre experts Abbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue plasminogen activator. |

For the modification scheme, the specific reason for modification and the details of the modification scheme should be explained. Here's a shipping process and some examples of checking for errors, you can refer but not copy:                                                                                                         example1:    Problem: The 'receiving package' activity with id='activity2' and the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway1' are in the wrong order and logic error.    Suggested modification: Move the 'receiving package' activity with id='activity2' under the branch with id='branch1' of the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway1'.    Reason: After the sender chooses to pick up the package, the Courier can perform the activities of accepting the package. example2:    Problem: The activities are not a parallel relation in the parallel gateway with id='parallelgateway1'.    Suggested modification: In the parallel gateway with id='parallelgateway1', change the activities from a parallel structure to a sequential structure.    Reason: Logically, 'packing and handling packages' and 'transport and distribution packages' are not parallel relationships. example3:    Problem: The 'end of service' activity with id='activity7' and the 'sign for parcel' activity with id='activity8' are in the wrong order.    Suggested modification: Swap the position of the 'end of service' activity with id='activity7' and the 'sign for parcel' activity with id='activity8'.    Reason: Only after the recipient sign for package can the express company terminate the service. example4:    Problem: The sequence gateway with id='sequencegateway1' is incorrectly formatted.    Suggested modification: Remove the gateway label of the sequence gateway with id='sequencegateway1'.    Reason: Sequential activities need not be contained under a gateway. When making recommendations, don't forget the format of the process text: {format} Please note: 1) If you think there is an error in the process text, output the error as requested. Patients typically\npresent at the ED, but the same practices should be followed at an outpatient clinic or when patients\nare directly admitted to acute care. The recommendations emphasize assessing the patient to inform\nclinical decision-making and determine the most appropriate pathway.\nModule 1 Recommended Practices \n1.1 Initial evaluation\n1.1.1 Rapid initial evaluation should be conducted for airway,\nbreathing, and circulation\n1.2 Initial examinations\n1.2.1 All patients should undergo a neurological examination to\ndetermine focal neurological deficits and assess stroke severity on a\nstandardized stroke scale (NIHSS or CNS for stroke)\n1.2.2 Patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute neurological\nsymptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom\nonset, and may be considered for those in whom treatment can be\ninitiated within a 12- hour window from stroke symptom onset, who\nmeet clinical and imaging criteria may be considered for endovascular\ntherapy\n1.2.3 All patients with suspected acute stroke (presenting within acute\nstroke treatment time window) should undergo rapid brain imaging\n(non-contrast CT or MRI) and CTA (Door to CT/CTA should be less\nthan twenty five minutes)\n1.2.4. For patients with acute ischemic stroke that are clinically eligible\nfor acute stroke treatments, advanced CT imaging including CT and\nmultiphase CTA or Perfusion CT should be considered.\n1.2.5 Brain imaging should be interpreted immediately by a health care\nprovider with expertise in reading/interpreting CT/CTA and/or MRI of\nthe brain\n1.2.6 All patients should undergo ECG to detect atrial fibrillation and\nother acute arrhythmias but should not delay acute treatment\n1.2.7 A chest x-ray should not delay assessment for thrombolysis \n1.2.8 All patients should have the following blood work:\n• CBC\n• electrolytes\n• creatinine\n• glucose\n• INR\n• partial thromboplastin time\n• troponin test (if clinically indicated)\n1.3 Assessment and early management of dysphagia\n1.3.1 All patients with stroke should be made NPO initially and have\ntheir swallowing ability screened using a simple, valid, reliable, bedside\ntesting protocol as part of their initial assessment and before initiating\noral medication, fluid, or food\n1.3.2 All patients with stroke who are not alert within the first 24 hours\nshould be monitored closely, and swallowing ability should be\nscreened when clinically appropriate\n1.3.3 Patients with stroke presenting with features indicating dysphagia\nor pulmonary aspiration should receive a full clinical assessment of\ntheir swallowing ability by a speech-language pathologist or\nappropriately trained specialist who would advise on swallowing ability\nand the required consistency of diet and fluids\n1.4 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies\n1.4.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged from the\nED, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary\nprevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)\n1.5 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic or ICH stroke patients\n1.6 Initial examinations for ischemic stroke\n1.6.1 Recommendation is not applicable to ischemic stroke patients\n1.6.2 All other patients (presenting beyond 48 hours) with a TIA or\nischemic stroke should undergo vascular imaging of the brain and\nneck arteries as soon as possible\n1.7 Initial examinations for ICH\n1.7.1 Patients with ICH must be treated as a medical emergency. ICH\nshould be promptly recognized, and patients should be evaluated\nimmediately by physicians with expertise in stroke management\n1.7.2 Evaluation of patients with acute ICH should include questions\nabout anticoagulant therapy, measurement of platelet count, PTT, and\nINR\n1.7.3 Select patients with ICH should have CTA or MRA as soon as\npossible\nAbbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke\nManagement; CBC, complete blood count; CSBPR, Canadian Stroke Best Practices Recommendations; CNS, Canadian Neurological Scale;\nCT, computerized tomography; CTA, computed tomography angiography; ECG, electrocardiogram; ED, emergency department;EVT,\nEndovascular Treatment ICH, intracerebral hemorrhage; INR, international normalized ratio; MRA, magnetic resonance angiography; MRI,\nmagnetic resonance imaging; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; NIHSS, National Institute of Health Stroke\nScale; NPO, nil per os (nothing by mouth); PTT, partial thromboplastin time; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient\nischemic attack.\n\nThe following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.\n\nModule 1 Implementation Considerations\nGeneral considerations\n• Development and/or updating of ED acute stroke protocols to ensure communication with CritiCall Ontario as soon as\npossible to determine treatment and transport decisions for patients presenting with potentially disabling, acute\nneurological symptoms suggestive of an acute stroke within 6.0 hours of symptom onset and considered to be a\npotential candidate for intravenous thrombolysis and/or endovascular therapy (EVT)\n• Regional planning for access to acute stroke EVT should consider the following:\no The importance of rapid triage, assessment and brain imaging (including parallel vascular imaging) of acute\nstroke patients who are potential candidates for EVT.\no Development of regional and provincial bypass and repatriation agreements\no Sites should establish rapid communication protocols with closest EVT site(s), facilitated through CritiCall\nOntario.\no Development of regional protocols to support a “drip and ship” process.\n• Capacity Planning is needed to develop access to EVT for patients living within regions where currently there is a\ngreater than two (2) hour patient transfer time to an EVT Centre\n• Where feasible, development of processes and protocols to facilitate EMS to divert patients to regional or district\nstroke and /Endovascular Treatment centres if there is suspicion of stroke\n• The process for EMS prenotification of the receiving stroke hospital about a stroke patient arrival should be better\nestablished to ensure acute stroke teams receive timely and detailed information\n• Collaboration between local EMS and institutions that provide stroke services should occur in all stroke networks\nacross the province to support quality improvement initiatives that facilitate access to stroke care\n• Ongoing education should be provided to EMS crews about the recognition of stroke symptoms and regional medical\nredirect protocols, including time sensitive treatments such as thrombolysis,EVT, patient transport monitoring and\nmanagement.\n• Upon receiving EMS prenotification, the receiving hospital’s acute stroke team should be contacted and called to the\nED (for appropriate patients)\n• Standardized stroke assessment and treatment protocols/tools should be developed and used in all Ontario hospital\nEDs\n• Access should be increased in all hospitals to either stroke physician experts or radiologists who can interpret brain\nimaging urgently and allow for full assessment and treatment with tPA thrombolysis within target door to needle time\nof less than 60 minutes in 90% of treated patients and a median door to needle time of 30 mins. of patient arrival for\nappropriate patients\n• Sufficient human resources capacity should be ensured so that patients can be diagnosed and treated in a timely\nmanner\n• To facilitate early assessment, hospital-level CTAS I or CTAS II access to diagnostic imaging should be established\nfor suspected stroke patients\n• Rapid turnaround times for lab work should be negotiated in hospitals that provide tPA thrombolysis for ischemic\nstroke patients to facilitate rapid assessment and treatment\n• Efforts to raise public awareness about the symptoms of stroke and when to contact 911 should continue to be\nenhanced and funded\n• Capacity planning to ensure access to CT/CTA should include:\no Training for CT technologists\no Adoption of provincial CT/CTA Protocol\no Human resource availability\no Communication processes with EVT Centre experts\nAbbreviations: CTAS, Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale; ED, emergency department; EMS, emergency medical services; tPA, tissue\nplasminogen activator.', 'suggestions': "Problem: The parallelGateway with id='parallelGateway1' is logically unreasonable because the downstream exclusiveGateway with id='exclusiveGateway5' can only be reached after both parallel branches are completed. This means the diagnosis-specific pathway based on brain imaging, especially the ICH emergency pathway, may be delayed by ECG, blood work, or chest x-ray activities in branch id='branch9'.\n\nSuggested modification: Move the exclusiveGateway with id='exclusiveGateway5' into the imaging branch id='branch1', placing it immediately after imaging interpretation activities id='activity6_1' and id='activity6_2'. Keep ECG, lab work, troponin decision, and chest x-ray in parallel as supportive tasks, but do not make completion of those tasks a prerequisite for entering the ICH or ischemic/TIA pathway.\n\nReason: According to the recommendations, brain imaging must be interpreted immediately, ICH must be treated as a medical emergency, and ECG/chest x-ray should not delay acute treatment. In the current design, the synchronization effect of parallelGateway1 incorrectly forces the process to wait for non-decisive parallel tasks before proceeding to urgent post-imaging clinical decisions.", 'format': "\nWhen the process starts, please output ''\nAn activity is represented as follows:\n\nThe exclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe parallel gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n", 'examples': ' '} |
